questionText: A curve has parametric equations $x = 1 + \sqrt{t}$ and $y = t$, where $t \geq 0$. Find a Cartesian equation of the curve, and state both the domain of $x$ and the range of $y$.

steps.explanation: To eliminate $t$ we first get the root by itself. Subtracting $1$ leaves $\sqrt{t}$ alone on one side.

steps.description: Use the sign of the root to fix the domain
steps.explanation: A real square root is never negative, so the right side $x - 1$ must also be non-negative. This is what will pin down the domain.
Squaring both sides undoes the square root and gives $t$ in terms of $x$, ready to substitute into $y$.
Since $y = t$, replacing $t$ gives $y$ directly in terms of $x$, eliminating the parameter.
This is a parabola with vertex at $(1,\,0)$, though the parametrisation only traces part of it.
From step 2, $x - 1 \geq 0$, so $x$ is never less than $1$: only the right-hand half of the parabola is traced.
A squared quantity is never negative, so $y$ can never drop below $0$.
The minimum $y = 0$ occurs at the left end of the domain, $x = 1$, which is the vertex of the parabola.
Starting from $0$ and increasing as $x$ grows, $y$ takes every value from $0$ upward, so the range is $y \geq 0$.

steps.description: Check the endpoint from the parameter
steps.explanation: At $t = 0$ the parametric equations give $(1,\,0)$, matching the corner of the domain and range.
The equation with its domain and range gives the complete description of the traced curve.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $y = (x - 1)^{2}$ with domain $x \geq 1$ and range $y \geq 0$ (the right-hand half of the parabola with vertex $(1,\,0)$).
